1: 'And the angel who talked with me came again and woke me, like a man who is awakened out of his sleep.' The fifth night vision is introduced with the unusual detail that the angel has to wake Zechariah — the prophet has fallen asleep between visions. The sleep-and-awakening frame recurs in Dan 8:18 and 10:9 — the visionary state is analogous to sleep, requiring divine arousal to receive the next vision. The angel's gentle waking contrasts with the urgency of the oracles.
2: 'And he said to me, What do you see? I said, I see, and behold, a lampstand all of gold, with a bowl on the top of it, and seven lamps on it, with seven lips on each of the lamps that are on the top of it.' The golden lampstand (menorah) with seven lamps — the structure of the tabernacle/temple menorah (Exod 25:31-40; 37:17-24). This is one of the central objects of Israel's worship, present in both the tabernacle and both temples. The seven lamps with their seven spouts (forty-nine total oil-conduits) indicate extraordinary and continuous illumination.
3: 'And there were two olive trees by it, one on the right of the bowl and the other on its left.' The two olive trees flanking the lampstand are a new element not in the tabernacle menorah — they supply oil directly to the lampstand without human intervention. The identification of the two trees will be given in vv. 11-14: they are the two sons of oil (Zerubbabel and Joshua), the civil and priestly leadership of the restoration. The self-feeding lampstand indicates a divinely sustained rather than humanly maintained divine presence.
4: 'And I said to the angel who talked with me, What are these, my lord?' Zechariah's standard question initiates the angelic interpretation. His repeated questioning throughout the night visions (1:9; 2:2; 4:4; 5:6; 6:4) is the structural device that draws out the oracular content. The prophet's incomprehension is not an embarrassment but a narrative requirement: it occasions the revelatory explanation.
5: 'Then the angel who talked with me answered and said to me, Do you not know what these are? I said, No, my lord.' The angel's counter-question — 'do you not know?' — is gentle irony. Even the prophet who is receiving these visions requires angelic interpretation. The admission 'No, my lord' (lo adoni) is an expression of appropriate prophetic humility before the mystery of divine revelation.
6: 'Then he said to me, This is the word of YHWH to Zerubbabel: Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit, says YHWH of hosts.' The most famous verse in Zechariah — the word to Zerubbabel and to all who would build the kingdom of God. The temple rebuilding will not succeed by military force (chayil) or human strength (koach) but by YHWH's Spirit (ruachi). This directly addresses the community's discouragement in the face of Persian opposition (Ezra 4-5) and limited resources. The Spirit-power theology connects to Hag 2:5 (my Spirit remains in your midst) and points forward to the NT's pneumatology.
7: 'Who are you, O great mountain? Before Zerubbabel you shall become a plain, and he shall bring forward the top stone amid shouts of Grace, grace to it!' The great mountain (har hagadol) that becomes a plain before Zerubbabel — the obstacles to the temple rebuilding reduced to nothing. The capstone (even haroshh) brought out with shouts of 'Grace! Grace!' (chen chen lah) — the community celebrating the final stone of the building with cries of beauty and divine favor. The mountain-to-plain imagery appears in Isa 40:4 and 45:2 (YHWH making the way straight before Cyrus).
8: 'Then the word of YHWH came to me, saying.' A secondary oracle embedded within the vision — YHWH speaks directly to Zechariah without angelic mediation at this point, confirming and extending the vision's message.
9: 'The hands of Zerubbabel have laid the foundation of this house; his hands shall also complete it. Then you will know that YHWH of hosts has sent me to you.' The specific promise to Zerubbabel: he who began the work will finish it. The foundation was laid under Sheshbazzar (Ezra 5:16) but the building stalled; Zerubbabel is restoring it. The messenger-formula ('you will know that YHWH has sent me') makes the completion of the temple the authentication of Zechariah's prophetic commission.
10: 'For whoever has despised the day of small things shall rejoice, and shall see the plumb line in the hand of Zerubbabel. These seven are the eyes of YHWH, which range through the whole earth.' Do not despise the day of small things (yom qetan ha'olam) — the modest beginnings of the second temple. The contrast with the first temple's glory (2:3) had caused discouragement; Zechariah reframes smallness as the mode of divine working. The seven eyes of YHWH ranging through the whole earth identify the seven lamps of the lampstand (v. 2) as YHWH's comprehensive omniscience — seeing all, missing nothing, present everywhere.
11: 'Then I said to him, What are these two olive trees on the right and the left of the lampstand?' Zechariah returns to the olive trees, the unexplained elements of the vision. The repeated question drives the revelation toward its climactic identification of the two anointed ones.
12: 'And a second time I answered and said to him, What are these two branches of the olive trees, which are beside the two golden pipes from which the golden oil is poured out?' The refinement of the question: not just the trees but the specific branches (shevelei hazayit) near the golden channels through which oil flows. The branches feeding the lamp directly suggest the leaders who channel divine provision to the community without the oil passing through human hands first.
13: 'He said to me, Do you not know what these are? I said, No, my lord.' The second admission of ignorance — emphasizing that the identity of the two anointed ones is not self-evident but requires divine revelation to understand.
14: 'Then he said, These are the two sons of oil who stand before the Lord of the whole earth.' The two sons of oil (benei hayitzhar, 'sons of fresh oil') — Zerubbabel (the Davidic civil governor) and Joshua (the Aaronic high priest). They stand before the Lord of the whole earth (adon kol ha'aretz) — the same title used in Josh 3:11, 13 at the Jordan crossing, emphasizing YHWH's universal sovereignty. The dual anointing — royal and priestly — points forward to the one who will hold both offices: the Branch who will be priest on his throne (Zech 6:13).
